items.title: Here's my conversation with Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA, the most valuable & one of the most influen...
items.content:
Here's my conversation with Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA, the most valuable & one of the most influential companies in the history of human civilization. It is the engine powering the AI revolution.

This was a fascinating & inspiring conversation, in parts super-technical on engineering of every part of the AI stack, memory, power, supply chain (TSMC, ASML, etc), in parts about leadership & psychology, and in parts personal & philosophical about life, consciousness, mortality, and human nature.

It's here on X in full and is up everywhere else (see comment).

Following the success of the EurIPS and NeurIPS-Mexico City pilots in 2025, we are thrilled to announce two official NeurIPS 2026 satellite events for this year!

These will be held in Paris, France and Atlanta, USA, respectively, running alongside the main venue in Sydney, Australia. 

Both satellite events will feature keynotes, oral and poster presentations of accepted NeurIPS 2026 papers, as well as workshops. We are planning tutorials, affinity events, and other elements for the satellite sites and we'll share more information as planning advances.

Wherever you choose to join us, the entire NeurIPS organizing committee is working hard to deliver an outstanding experience for the whole community!

items.title: We recently worked with The Yomiuri Shimbun to analyze more than a million social media posts to map...
items.content:
We recently worked with The Yomiuri Shimbun to analyze more than a million social media posts to map out state-sponsored information campaigns.

https://t.co/fBQQLg1glS

Keyword searches are fragile for modern OSINT. To fix this, our team used an ensemble of different LLMs combined with our Novelty Search algorithm to extract underlying narratives purely from context. (e.g., The system successfully mapped posts demanding "a politician retract a statement" to the broader, hidden narrative of "Taiwan interference").

The system clusters these granular narratives hierarchically and generates testable hypotheses, citing specific evidence.

Human journalists took the AI-generated hypotheses, interviewed real-world government sources, and verified the timeline of the coordinated campaign our system uncovered. Fascinating look at human-AI collaboration for intelligence analysis.

items.title:
AGI has been achieved in Ireland.

Artificial Guinness Intelligence.

Engineer Matt Cortland built a...
items.content:
AGI has been achieved in Ireland.

Artificial Guinness Intelligence.

Engineer Matt Cortland built an AI voice agent named Rachel, gave her a Northern Irish accent, and pointed her at every pub in the country. 

Over St. Paddy's weekend, she rang 3,000+ of them to ask one question: how much for a pint of Guinness?

How he built it: ElevenLabs for the voice, Twilio and an old Irish SIM to place the calls, Google Places API to map 5,200+ pubs across all 32 counties, and Claude to parse the transcripts for prices.

2,052 picked up. Barely any even realized she was AI. The whole operation ran him about €200.

The result is a live price index he's calling the Guinndex.

Ireland's statistics office used to track pint prices, but stopped in 2011. 

An engineer with a weekend and a voice agent just picked up where they left off.

items.content:
The idea that technology, like AI, deskills us is not a surprise. I learned cursive at school, my father learned how to use a slide rule. Neither skill is widely mourned.

What is important is whether we will make deliberate choices about what skills to keep & which they will be.

items.title:
Every large company will eventually ban vibe-coding.

Vibe-coding is now generating as much technica...
items.content:
Every large company will eventually ban vibe-coding.

Vibe-coding is now generating as much technical debt as 10 regular developers in half the time.

Vibe-coding is awesome for a first draft, but you can't expect to push AI slop to production and not destroy your software over time.

Producing code is no longer a bottleneck.

Testing that code, debugging it, monitoring it in production, and fixing it when it breaks is where everyone is spending their time.

We've 10x'd the speed of writing code, but we are still in the Stone Age with everything that happens after the code is written.

Here is a very cool tool tackling this:

You can build "AI Production Engineers" using PlayerZero and make them work for you.

These are agents that do this:

• Simulate how your code will work in production
• Diagnose issues when they happen
• Learn from every incident so it doesn't happen again

This is pretty awesome!

These agents simulate code behavior against real production data. They use actual customer behavior, historical incidents, and edge cases without writing a single test script.

When something breaks, the agent traces the issue to the exact line of code and PR, generates the fix, and routes it to the right engineer.

And every bug these agents solve serves as training data to improve the system.

Here is a link to check them out:
https://t.co/JWsbNp0hTo

Thanks to the Player Zero team for partnering with me on this post.

Last week, Sierra released 𝜏³-Bench, an update to our industry standard agent benchmark, expanding the benchmark in several important ways:

𝜏-Knowledge tests whether agents can operate over large collections of internal company documents spread across systems and formats;

𝜏-Voice evaluates agents built for live voice conversations; and

We’ve also incorporated fixes contributed by the 𝜏-Bench community across existing 𝜏-Bench domains to improve evaluation accuracy.

Read more here: https://t.co/wBZ4ImNoII

items.title:
You can now teach a humanoid robot tennis without showing it a single real match.

LATENT is a new p...
items.content:
You can now teach a humanoid robot tennis without showing it a single real match.

LATENT is a new paper from Tsinghua University. It skips recording perfect game footage entirely.

Instead, it uses short, messy clips of basic moves. 

A forehand swing here, a sidestep there. Just pieces, not full matches.

A two-layer system does the heavy lifting:
1. The bottom layer watches clips and learns individual moves. 

2. The top layer figures out when to use which move and fixes mistakes on the fly.

It runs on a Unitree G1 robot. The machine holds rallies with real humans. 

Balls fly in at 15-30 m/s. It reacts within milliseconds.

What made this possible:
> No clean training data required
> No real match recordings needed
> No human remote-controlling the robot
> Works outside simulation too

The same approach could work for soccer and parkour. Any sport where collecting clean data is nearly impossible.

items.title:
Why aren't more people talking about Replit right now?

I started using Replit consistently late las...
items.content:
Why aren't more people talking about Replit right now?

I started using Replit consistently late last year when they released Agent 3. It was my way to "let go" as a developer and build things without worrying about the code.

It's actually been a good way to teach my kid "web development".

Replit just launched Agent 4, and I think it's massively, massively underhyped.

Best thing: You can now run multiple agents in parallel.

• Old agent: You give it a task, and you have to wait for it to finish before you can do anything else.

• New agent: Do this, and that, and this. All of it at the same time.

It feels like going from a Toyota to a Lexus.

I can now run an agent to build my frontend, another agent to build my backend, and a third agent to write test cases.

By the way, they also have an infinite design canvas for UI-related work, so you can also do design at the same time as everything else.

items.title: @Noahpinion Solving particular *problems* in Math or theoretical Physics usually requires a very hig...
items.content: @Noahpinion Solving particular *problems* in Math or theoretical Physics usually requires a very high level of familiarity with similar problems and techniques. The problem in modern math/physics is that it's very hard for any individual to haver a wide enough learning to be able to sift through all of the available literature efficiently and effectively. Exactly the kind of stuff that AI can be trined to do.

@davidfano I appreciate that, but I am not unique at Anthropic at all.

Everyone here works hard every day to serve our customers and cares so deeply about the work and the mission.

This is one of the fastest growing businesses of all time in one of the most competitive markets ever. We all have to make fast decisions with imperfect information, but I have seen everyone here work with the best of intentions.

That said, this work is very important and I hope everyone continues to hold us to a high standard. We may not always meet it, but we are always striving to do so.

items.title:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the ed...
items.content:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.
Closer to the device.
Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon discusses AI inference shifting from centralized to distributed/edge computing, comparing to how video distribution evolved
items.reasoning: Good strategic insight about edge AI inference and the analogy to video CDN evolution. Relevant trend analysis.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon discusses AI inference shifting from centralized to distributed/edge computing, comparing to how video distribution evolved
items.content_html:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.

Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.

Closer to the device.

Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.

items.content:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI
→ AI agents
→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.

items.title:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity...
items.content:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity
→ Secure data flows
→ Low latency

If your network drops, lags, or gets compromised, the AI fails.

This is why “AI-ready” is not just about GPUs.

It is about differentiated connectivity, powered by:

→ Network APIs
→ Network slicing
→ Edge execution

items.content:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Queries get faster.
Dashboards recover.

Everyone moves on.

But at scale, every new index quietly multiplies your write load.

This is the index trap I discussed with @TigerDatabase.

Tiger Data's deep dive on the optimization treadmill covers the full picture: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

#TigerDataPartner

items.title:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, A...
items.content:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, AI telemetry, financial feeds all share the same pattern:

→ continuous ingestion
→ append-only data
→ time-based queries
→ massive retention

Eventually the architecture matters more than tuning.

Tiger Data published the full breakdown here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

Check out the full article : https://t.co/X118MPO3Bu

Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be trav...
items.content:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be traversed for each insert
→ Time-series data hits the rightmost index pages
→ Autovacuum activity increases as tables grow

The result is a predictable treadmill.

Add index → queries faster
Write load increases → latency rises
Scale infra → repeat

Tiger Data’s deep dive on this pattern: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.content:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1 write per index

So if you have:

→ 5 indexes → 6 writes per insert

At scale:

50k inserts/sec → 300k writes/sec
100k inserts/sec → 600k writes/sec

And that’s before maintenance.

Tiger Data explains the full impact here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + ti...
items.content:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + timestamp
→ Partial indexes for recent data
→ Covering indexes for hot queries

Result → queries drop back to milliseconds.

But something important just changed.

Every insert now got more expensive.

Tiger Data breaks this down in detail here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title: Aave is a decentralized finance protocol that enables users to lend and borrow cryptocurrencies via ...
items.content:
Aave is a decentralized finance protocol that enables users to lend and borrow cryptocurrencies via liquidity pools, where smart contracts manage interest rates and collateral, creating a transparent and automated system without intermediaries. 

Microblog @antgrasso #DeFi #web3

Aave operates through liquidity pools where users deposit digital assets and receive interest that adjusts dynamically based on supply and demand within each pool, while borrowers access funds by providing overcollateralized positions that protect the system from volatility and ensure stability.

The protocol introduces flash loans, which allow instant borrowing within a single transaction when repaid immediately, opening advanced use cases in arbitrage and refinancing.

The AAVE token supports governance decisions, staking in the Safety Module, and contributes to protocol security during shortfall events.

A non-custodial architecture ensures that users maintain control of their assets at all times, interacting directly with smart contracts.

Its presence across Ethereum and Layer 2 networks improves accessibility and transaction efficiency, supporting broader adoption and practical use in decentralized finance.

The protocol introduces flash loans, which allow instant borrowing within a single transaction when repaid immediately, opening advanced use cases in arbitrage and refinancing.

The AAVE token supports governance decisions, staking in the Safety Module, and contributes to protocol security during shortfall events.

A non-custodial architecture ensures that users maintain control of their assets at all times, interacting directly with smart contracts.

Its presence across Ethereum and Layer 2 networks improves accessibility and transaction efficiency, supporting broader adoption and practical use in decentralized finance.

items.title:
✈️ Another fatal crash in the US allegedly caused by ATC

An 🇨🇦 Air Canada plane crashed into a fire...
items.content:
✈️ Another fatal crash in the US allegedly caused by ATC

An 🇨🇦 Air Canada plane crashed into a fire truck at New York's LaGuardia Airport (LGA), two pilots died, but luckily most people survived

Just a week ago at Newark Liberty Airport, another one of New York City's main airports, two aircraft almost collided: an Alaska Airlines flight was told to immediately do a go-around (abort landing) after almost crashing into a FedEx plane that was landing at an intersecting runway

And just a year ago in Washington D.C., an American Airlines plane crashed into a Black Hawk helicopter killing all 64 people on board the plane and all 3 soldiers in the helicopter. The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) concluded that the ATC was hindered by "unsustainable traffic load" and only one ATC controller was managing both helicopter and plane traffic at the same time (among other systemic failures)

Air Canada, Alaska Airlines and American Airlines are some of the safest airlines in the world, and in general US airline are one of the safest in the world. American pilots are rigorous with safety and there's lots of stories with American pilots "rejecting the plane" and won't take off if they're not fully sure it's 100% safe to fly, as it should be

But the problem in the US now isn't the pilots, increasingly it's the air traffic control (ATC) being understaffed, overworked and some say possibly a quality problem of hiring the wrong people and/or not training them properly

That's very scary because even if you have a great plane, with great pilots, who do their best to fly safely, they're highly dependent on ATC to do their job to not crash into other planes

And if nobody can rely on ATC anymore, well, more people will certainly die in the future

items.content_html:
@nicolaibaaring @airtransat "By the late 1990s, having gained as much IP protection as possible for elements of the game such as the dimensions of the playing field and the shapes of the blocks, The Tetris Company was gaining attention due to its attempts to remove freeware and shareware versions of Tetris from the market by claiming both trade mark and copyright infringement. In response, the creators of Tetris copycat games have claimed that The Tetris Company had no legal basis to restrict the games, as long as they did not infringe on the Tetris trademark.

In the years since, on the whole The Tetris Company has been successful in its attempts to use its trade marks and copyrights to take down copycat games that have made it onto the various electronic markets. In August 2008, Apple Inc. removed “Tris”, a version of Tetris written for the iPhone without authorisation. The Tetris Company sued BioSocia in March 2009 to force them to remove the game “Blockles” from its website and, in May 2010, Google removed 35 games which were available for Android phones, at the request of The Tetris Company."

https://t.co/dI0HUgvl2I

items.title:
I'm looking for IP advice. Any IP specialists out there?

The question is the following. I was flyin...
items.content:
I'm looking for IP advice. Any IP specialists out there?

The question is the following. I was flying @airtransat the other day, and they offered online games onboard. One of these games was strangely similar to Tetris but called Trixology: https://t.co/I2LH6dIMNR

All chatbots say that The Tetris Company, Inc. is very anal in enforcing Tetris IP rights. And it's not enough to simply rename the game to have the right to sell it.

The Question: Is Trixology different enough from Tetris so that an airline could not be afraid of a lawsuit from The Tetris Company? Obviously, they haven't purchased the right to offer this game during the flight from The Tetris Company; otherwise, the game would be called Tetris and not Trixology.
